headline: Charles M. Russell — The Cowboy Artist of Great Falls
summary:
## Painting the West from a Studio on Fourth Street North

Charles Marion Russell arrived in Montana as a teenager in 1880, drawn by the romance of the open range. He worked as a wrangler and night herder in the Judith Basin before settling permanently in Great Falls in 1897, where he built the log-cabin studio on Fourth Street North that became his workshop and legend for nearly three decades.

Russell produced more than 2,000 oil and watercolor paintings, thousands of drawings, and scores of bronze sculptures—all celebrating the cowboys, Native peoples, and landscapes of the vanishing frontier West. His works combined technical mastery with deep affection for his subjects; he had lived alongside Blackfoot people and understood their ways in ways few white artists of his era did. His advocacy helped pass the legislation that created the Rocky Boy Reservation in 1916.

His wife Nancy managed his career with fierce effectiveness, negotiating sales, organizing exhibitions, and eventually building his international reputation with shows in New York, London, and beyond. The couple's 1926 home on Fourth Street, the

house, still stands adjacent to the C. M. Russell Museum complex, which today holds the world's largest collection of his original works.

Russell died in Great Falls on October 24, 1926. His funeral procession drew the entire city; schoolchildren were released from class to line the streets. The day the cowboy artist died, Great Falls lost one of its defining voices—and gained an enduring monument to Western American art.

headline: Paris Gibson — Visionary Founder Who Built Great Falls on a Grid
summary:
## From a Maine Farm to the Falls of the Missouri

Paris Gibson was born in Brownfield, Maine, on July 1, 1830, and graduated from Bowdoin College in 1851. After business failures in Minnesota, he traveled west in 1880 and stood at the edge of the Great Falls of the Missouri River. He saw not a scenic wonder but a power source capable of driving an industrial city, and spent the next decade making that vision real.

Gibson convinced his friend, railroad magnate James J. Hill, to extend his rail lines through the new townsite. In 1883 the city of Great Falls was incorporated, with Gibson serving as its first mayor. He designed the town along a precise grid—wide streets, logical blocks—that still defines the city's navigable layout today. By 1887, Hill's rails linked Great Falls to Helena and Butte, anchoring its position as the commercial center of north-central Montana.

Gibson's ambitions extended beyond commerce. He was a delegate to Montana's constitutional convention in 1889 and was appointed to fill a U.S. Senate vacancy in 1901, subsequently elected to serve a full term, representing Montana as a Democrat until 1905. Even after leaving Washington he remained in Great Falls, spending his final years there until his death on December 16, 1920.

The city he planned from bare prairie endures as a testament to one man's capacity to see potential where others saw only wilderness. His grave at Highland Cemetery lies within sight of the Missouri River that first captured his imagination.

headline: Mike Mansfield — From the Mines of Butte to the Senate Floor
summary:
## A Great Falls Boyhood That Shaped America's Longest Senate Leader

Mike Mansfield was born in New York City on March 16, 1903, to Irish immigrant parents. When his mother died and his father was unable to care for him, young Mike and his siblings moved to Great Falls, Montana, around 1910, to be raised by an aunt and uncle. The Missouri River town shaped his character—its working-class ethos, its ethnic immigrant neighborhoods, its blue-collar directness.

At fourteen he lied about his age and enlisted in the Navy, then served in the Army and the Marines, which sent him to the Philippines and China. Those years kindled a lifelong fascination with Asia that would define his statecraft. Returning to Montana, he worked as a mucker in the copper mines of Butte before earning a college degree and eventually a master's in history. He joined the faculty at the University of Montana in Missoula.

Elected to the U.S. House in 1942 and the Senate in 1952, Mansfield rose to become Senate Majority Leader in 1961—a position he held for sixteen years, the longest tenure in Senate history. He guided the Civil Rights Act of 1964 through a historic filibuster and advised presidents on foreign policy from Roosevelt through Ford.

After leaving the Senate in 1977, President Carter appointed him U.S. Ambassador to Japan, where he served until 1988. Mansfield died on October 5, 2001, and was buried at Arlington Cemetery. His tombstone reads simply: *PVT, US Marine Corps*—the rank he prized above all others.

headline: Einar Malmstrom — The Pilot Great Falls Immortalized in Steel and Concrete
summary:
## A Short Tenure That Left a Permanent Name on Montana's Skyline

Colonel Einar Axel Malmstrom was born in 1907 and rose through the ranks of the U.S. Army Air Forces to become one of the more decorated combat pilots of World War II. Flying a fighter escort mission over Europe, he was shot down on his 58th combat sortie and spent much of the war as a prisoner at Stalag Luft I South Compound in Barth, Germany, eventually becoming the senior American commander at the camp.

After liberation and repatriation, Malmstrom returned to active duty. In 1954 he was assigned as vice wing commander at Great Falls Air Force Base—a Cold War hub that had grown from a wartime B-17 training field into a major strategic installation. His tenure was brief but impactful: he was admired by the local community for his accessibility, his energy, and his genuine interest in the people of Great Falls.

On August 21, 1954, Malmstrom died when the T-33 Shooting Star trainer he was piloting crashed approximately one mile west of the Great Falls Municipal Airport. The loss struck the city hard. Local residents and civic leaders launched a campaign to rename the base in his honor. On October 1, 1955, Great Falls Air Force Base was officially redesignated Malmstrom Air Force Base.

The base went on to become the nation's first Minuteman ICBM installation in 1962 and remains today one of three active Minuteman III missile wings. Every time the name appears on a map, a shipping label, or a news dispatch, it carries the memory of a pilot Great Falls refused to forget.

headline: A. B. Guthrie Jr. — Pulitzer Laureate of the Big Sky
summary:
## Writing the West from the Shadow of the Rocky Mountain Front

Alfred Bertram "Bud" Guthrie Jr. was born in Indiana in 1901 but arrived in Montana at six months old when his father became the first principal of Choteau's high school. He grew up exploring the vast grasslands and mountain foothills that would later fill his fiction with aching authenticity. After earning a journalism degree from the University of Montana and spending two decades as a newspaper editor in Kentucky, he returned to Montana—splitting his time between Choteau and Great Falls.

His 1947 novel *The Big Sky* redefined the mountain-man Western, stripping away romanticism and replacing it with hard beauty and harder choices. The follow-up, *The Way West* (1949), won the Pulitzer Prize for Fiction in 1950, tracing the brutal realities of the Oregon Trail emigration. His screenplay for *Shane* (1953) earned an Academy Award nomination and proved his storytelling translated seamlessly to the screen.

Guthrie used Great Falls as his city base—libraries, publishers, contacts—while the solitude of his Choteau cabin and his land near the mountain front fed the landscapes of his imagination. He became an outspoken environmentalist in later decades, donating eighty acres to the Nature Conservancy's Pine Butte Preserve.

He died at his home west of Choteau on April 26, 1991, having done more than any other writer to capture Montana's territorial-era spirit on the page. The nickname he gave the state—"The Big Sky Country"—became official state shorthand, printed on every license plate in Montana.

headline: Monte Dolack — Great Falls-Born Artist of the Natural World
summary:
## Posters, Prints, and a Deep Love for Western Wildlife

Monte Dolack was born in Great Falls, Montana, in 1950 and found his artistic calling early, eventually establishing himself as one of the most widely recognized visual artists in the American West. His signature style—lush, saturated, often humorous depictions of wildlife in improbable situations—earned him national and international recognition from galleries, conservation organizations, and collectors.

Dolack studied art formally and built a prolific studio practice, producing posters, prints, and paintings that placed wild animals in juxtaposition with human environments in ways that managed to be simultaneously whimsical and pointed. His work has appeared in the Smithsonian and in major museum collections, and his posters for Montana conservation campaigns and cultural events became iconic within the state.

His connection to Great Falls remained deep throughout his life. In December 2001, he created a major new work—a 23-by-34-inch piece depicting Lewis and Clark and the Corps of Discovery at the White Cliffs of the Missouri—and donated it to the Lewis and Clark Interpretive Center near Black Eagle Dam, just outside the city where he was born. The image was used in fundraising campaigns for the center, tying his art directly to Great Falls' most celebrated stretch of river.

Dolack's legacy as a Great Falls native who built his art around the landscapes and wild creatures of the Northern Rockies places him in the lineage of C. M. Russell—a lineage of artists who found in Montana's natural world both subject matter and spiritual sustenance.

headline: Myrna Loy — Hollywood's "Perfect Wife" Who Began in the Big Sky
summary:
## From Helena's Radersburg Valley to the Silver Screen

Myrna Adele Williams was born on August 2, 1905, in Helena, Montana, though her family roots extended into the farming communities south of the city. She spent her early years in rural Radersburg before her family relocated to Helena proper, and later moved to California after her father's death in 1918. As she rose to stardom, Montana's wide horizons remained part of her self-understanding.

Renaming herself Myrna Loy for the screen, she appeared in dozens of silent films before finding her defining persona in the sound era. Her chemistry with William Powell in *The Thin Man* (1934) created one of Hollywood's most beloved screen couples. She was nominated for no competitive Academy Awards during her peak years—a snub historians and critics have long found baffling—but received an honorary Oscar in 1991.

Beyond film, Loy was a committed activist who worked for the American Red Cross, UNESCO, and civil rights causes. She was one of the first Hollywood stars to speak out against McCarthyism. She served on the National Committee Against Discrimination in Housing and lobbied Congress for fair housing legislation.

Loy died in New York City on December 14, 1993. The Myrna Loy Center for the Performing Arts in Helena—housed in the former Lewis and Clark County Jail—carries her name as a tribute to the state's most celebrated actress. Her life traced a path from Montana's frontier landscape to the center of twentieth-century American culture.

headline: Jeannette Rankin — Great Falls' Adopted Fighter for Peace and Suffrage
summary:
## The First Woman in Congress Found Her Political Home on the High Plains

Jeannette Rankin was born near Missoula, Montana, in 1880 but her political career and deepest public roots were forged in Great Falls and across Montana's First Congressional District. After earning a degree in biology and working as a social worker in Seattle and New York, she returned to Montana in 1910 to lead the state's suffrage campaign, crisscrossing the plains and the mountains by train and wagon.

In 1916 she was elected to the U.S. House of Representatives—the first woman ever to serve in Congress, four years before women had the constitutional right to vote nationwide. Based in her Great Falls district office during her congressional terms, she tackled labor rights, women's suffrage, and child welfare legislation. She cast one of the fifty votes against U.S. entry into World War I in 1917.

Defeated in a Senate bid and absent from Congress for two decades, she returned in 1940 and on December 8, 1941, cast the lone vote against declaring war on Japan after the attack on Pearl Harbor. The vote ended her congressional career but became the act for which she is most remembered—a principled, solitary stand against the tide.

Rankin spent her later years as an antiwar activist, leading a march of five thousand women against the Vietnam War in Washington in 1968, at age eighty-seven. She died in 1973. A bronze statue of her stands in the U.S. Capitol's National Statuary Hall, representing Montana—the state whose wide-open politics gave a woman a seat in Congress before almost anywhere else on Earth.

headline: William Andrews Clark — Copper King Whose Smelter Electrified Great Falls
summary:
## The Senator Who Helped Wire the World from a Bluff Above the Missouri

William Andrews Clark was born in Pennsylvania in 1839 and made his first fortune in the silver and copper mines beneath Butte Hill. One of Montana's three so-called Copper Kings—alongside Marcus Daly and F. Augustus Heinze—Clark was as much a businessman-politician as a miner, owning newspapers, railroads, banks, and the smelter at Black Eagle that became Great Falls' largest employer.

Clark's Boston and Montana Consolidated Copper and Silver Mining Company erected its first smelter in Black Eagle in 1890, drawn by the hydroelectric power of the Great Falls dams on the Missouri River. By the time the Anaconda company absorbed the operation in 1915, the Black Eagle smelter employed more than a thousand men—immigrants from Croatia, Italy, Norway, Greece, and beyond—and its towering stack had become the visual emblem of the city.

Clark's Senate career was one of the most tainted in Montana history. He openly bribed state legislators to win a Senate seat in 1899, resigned in disgrace when the U.S. Senate moved to expel him, then won re-election in 1901 and served until 1907. His war with Marcus Daly over the state capital location—ultimately won by Helena—consumed millions of dollars and defined Montana's Gilded Age politics.

Despite his infamy, Clark's investment in Great Falls infrastructure was transformative. The smelter he built produced the copper wire that helped electrify American cities and supplied essential materials through two world wars. His legacy in Great Falls is inseparable from the electric city it became.

headline: Wilbur F. Sanders — Helena's First Senator Who Helped Build the Rule of Law
summary:
## The Vigilante Prosecutor Who Became Montana's Constitutional Conscience

Wilbur Fisk Sanders was born in New York State in 1834 and arrived in Montana Territory in 1863 as a young lawyer accompanying his uncle, territorial governor Sidney Edgerton. He reached Bannack at precisely the moment the road agent gang of Henry Plummer was terrorizing the gold camps, and stepped forward to prosecute the murderer George Ives before the miners' court in December 1863—an act of extraordinary courage that established him as the territory's leading legal voice.

Sanders moved with the territorial capital from Bannack to Virginia City and eventually to Helena in the 1870s, where he built a three-story home that still stands. He served as corresponding secretary of the Society of Montana Pioneers, helped found the Montana Bar Association, and for twenty-five years was president of the Montana Historical Society. His law practice included years as regional counsel for the Northern Pacific Railroad.

When Montana achieved statehood in 1889, the newly convened legislature elected Sanders as one of Montana's first two U.S. Senators. He served the short term, from January 1890 to March 1893, bringing Helena's establishment Republican politics to Washington at the moment the state was finding its constitutional footing.

Sanders died in Helena on July 7, 1905, and was interred at Forestvale Cemetery. A bronze statue of him stands in the Montana State Capitol rotunda—silent testimony to a man who helped transform a territory built on gold and chaos into something resembling ordered governance.

headline: The Five Great Falls: Lewis and Clark's Epic Portage of 1805
summary:
## Eighteen Miles of Struggle at the Heart of the Continent

In late May 1805, the Corps of Discovery reached the roar of falling water and knew they had found the Great Falls of the Missouri that Native informants had described. What they had not been told was that the falls were not one cascade but five successive cataracts spanning eighteen miles of canyon—a revelation that turned a planned half-day portage into a brutal three-week ordeal.

Meriwether Lewis first viewed the largest fall on June 13, 1805, scrambling to the top of a rocky bluff above the Missouri to gaze at what he called "the grandest sight I ever beheld." The main fall dropped roughly eighty feet and was flanked by additional cascades at Black Eagle, Crooked Falls, Rainbow Falls, and Colter Falls. The Corps built crude wagons from cottonwood and hauled their heavy dugout canoes and equipment over cactus-studded prairie in temperatures that swung from searing heat to violent hailstorms.

The men wore out their moccasins, suffered cactus wounds to their feet, and endured relentless grizzly bear encounters. Captain Clark mapped the portage route meticulously, and those maps became crucial documents of western geography. Sacagawea, gravely ill during the portage, recovered enough to continue west. The Corps cached their largest boat—an iron-framed vessel they had hauled from Illinois—after it failed to hold pitch in the dry Montana air.

The Great Falls Portage was designated a National Historic Landmark in 1966. The Lewis and Clark National Historic Trail Interpretive Center, built on a bluff above the Missouri in Great Falls, preserves the story and overlooks the same cottonwood-lined river the Corps navigated more than two centuries ago.

headline: Paris Gibson Founds Great Falls: A City Planned from Scratch in 1883
summary:
## When a Maine Businessman Staked a City on Hydroelectric Power

In the summer of 1880, Paris Gibson stood at the edge of the Missouri River and looked at the five cascades that gave the site its name. While others saw scenery, Gibson saw shaft horsepower. Convincing his friend James J. Hill to back a townsite company and extend his rail line through the new city, Gibson spent the next three years assembling land, recruiting settlers, and designing a municipal grid of exceptional clarity.

Great Falls was formally incorporated in 1883 with Gibson as its first mayor. The city's street plan—wide numbered avenues running east-west, lettered streets north-south—was laid out with the precision of an engineer's compass, making the growing frontier town unusually navigable. Gibson actively recruited immigrant workers and marketed the city to eastern investors as a coming industrial hub.

The timing proved right. The Great Northern Railway's main line reached Great Falls in 1887, linking it to Helena, Butte, and eventually the Pacific Coast. The railroad brought the Boston and Montana Consolidated Copper and Silver Mining Company, which erected its first smelter in Black Eagle in 1890. Within a decade of incorporation, Great Falls had a smelter, multiple dams under construction, and a population in the thousands.

Gibson remained the city's civic patriarch, serving in the U.S. Senate from 1901 to 1905 and living in Great Falls until his death in 1920. The city he laid out with a ruler and a vision endures today as Montana's third-largest, its streets still running on the grid he drew.

headline: The Black Eagle Smelter: How Great Falls Became the Electric City
summary:
## Copper, Steam, and the 506-Foot Stack That Defined a Skyline

In 1890, the Boston and Montana Consolidated Copper and Silver Mining Company chose a bluff overlooking the Missouri River just north of Great Falls to build Montana's largest smelter. The location was no accident: the Great Falls dams could supply cheap hydroelectric power, and the Northern Pacific and Great Northern railroads could carry copper ore from Butte and refined wire to eastern markets.

By 1908, the construction of the 506-foot smokestacks had begun—the tallest structure in Montana at the time, visible for thirty miles across the high plains. The operation refined copper into the wire that helped electrify American cities, supplied essential materials in both World Wars, and at its peak employed more than four thousand workers in the smelter and the adjacent community of Black Eagle.

The Anaconda Copper Mining Company absorbed the Black Eagle operation from Boston and Montana in 1915. Workers came from Croatia, Italy, Norway, Greece, and a dozen other nations, building a multicultural neighborhood of bars, churches, and company housing in the shadow of the stack. The Black Eagle Dam, completed in 1891, fed power directly to the smelter, and it was this connection that earned Great Falls the enduring nickname "the Electric City."

The smelter closed in 1980 amid labor strikes and falling copper prices. On September 19, 1981, six hundred pounds of dynamite brought the great stack down in thirty seconds—an event that drew thousands to the riverbank to watch a century of industrial history collapse into rubble. The Anaconda superfund cleanup continues on the former 405-acre site today.

headline: Ryan Dam and the Hydroelectric Chain That Powered a City
summary:
## Building the Missouri's Power Staircase, One Dam at a Time

The Missouri River drops more than five hundred feet as it tumbles through the canyon at Great Falls, a hydrological gift that Paris Gibson recognized in 1880 and that engineers spent the following four decades harnessing. The result was a cascade of five dams—Black Eagle, Rainbow, Cochrane, Ryan, and Morony—that made Great Falls one of the most intensively hydroelectrified stretches of river in the American West.

Ryan Dam, built on the largest of the five falls and completed in 1915, was the crown of the project. At 1,336 feet long and 61 feet high, with a six-unit powerhouse on the left bank, it occupied what Lewis and Clark had called "Grand Fall"—the same 87-foot cascade they had marveled at in June 1805. The dam's reservoir stretches seven miles upstream, and its six turbines still generate 72 megawatts. Contemporary newspapers called it likely the largest and most modern hydroelectric plant west of the Mississippi.

Black Eagle Dam had come first, completed in 1891 primarily to supply direct current to the Boston and Montana smelter. Rainbow Dam followed in 1910. Together, the five dams formed a continuous power staircase that supported not just the copper industry but the city's growing residential and commercial grid. Montana Power Company, a direct descendant of the Anaconda smelter's power operations, managed the system through most of the twentieth century.

Today the dams remain operational under NorthWestern Corporation. Ryan Dam sits within sight of a popular island picnic area where visitors can see—just barely—the remnant cascades that once made this one of the most dramatic river sights on the continent.

headline: Malmstrom Air Force Base: From Lend-Lease Hub to ICBM Fortress
summary:
## How Great Falls Became America's First Minuteman Missile Command

In June 1942, with war raging in Europe and the Pacific, construction began on a U.S. Army Air Corps base six miles east of Great Falls. The facility's initial mission was to serve as the American terminus of the Northwest Staging Route—the air corridor through Canada and Alaska by which thousands of combat aircraft were transferred to the Soviet Union under the Lend-Lease program. Over twenty-one months, approximately 8,000 aircraft passed through Great Falls bound for Soviet pilots on the Eastern Front.

After World War II the base evolved through multiple missions: Berlin Airlift crew training, Cold War air defense, and Strategic Air Command operations. On October 1, 1955, it was renamed Malmstrom Air Force Base in honor of Colonel Einar Malmstrom, the popular vice wing commander who had died in a 1954 training crash near the base.

The most consequential transformation came in 1959, when the Air Force Ballistic Missile Committee selected Malmstrom to host the nation's first Minuteman ICBM complex. Construction of launch silos began across the central Montana plains in March 1961. By July 1962 the first Minuteman I missile was operational at Launch Facility Alpha-09—making Great Falls the nucleus of America's ground-based nuclear deterrent.

Today the 341st Missile Wing at Malmstrom controls 150 Minuteman III ICBMs scattered across a 13,800-square-mile area of Montana prairie. The base remains the largest of three Minuteman wings in the country and Great Falls' largest employer, a Cold War institution that shows no sign of standing down.

headline: The Blackfeet and the Missouri: Ancient Ties to the Falls Country
summary:
## Long Before the Dams, the Niitsitapi Called This River Home

For hundreds of years before Paris Gibson drew his grid or the Great Northern Railroad laid its tracks, the Great Falls of the Missouri River fell through the territory of the Niitsitapi—the Blackfoot Confederacy. The Piegan Blackfeet (Amsskáápipikani) ranged across the high plains north of the Missouri, following the buffalo herds that sustained their culture and economy.

The falls themselves were a landmark in Blackfoot oral geography, known as a place of power and spiritual significance. Lewis and Clark's 1805 journals document encounters with Blackfoot people along the Marias River, less than fifty miles north of the falls, and the expedition's tense confrontation with a Piegan raiding party on the return journey in 1806 left one of the few violent episodes of the entire two-year trek.

As the fur trade era gave way to the reservation era, Blackfoot territory contracted sharply. A series of treaties—culminating in the establishment of the Blackfeet Reservation in northern Montana in 1855—confined the Niitsitapi to land east and north of the present-day Rocky Mountain Front. The Lame Bull Treaty of 1855 set the southern boundary of the Blackfeet Reservation and effectively ceded the falls country to the United States.

The Blackfeet Nation today, headquartered in Browning, Montana, maintains cultural and historical ties to the Great Falls region. The Lewis and Clark Interpretive Center in Great Falls acknowledges the Blackfoot presence that pre-dated every monument, dam, and museum in the city by centuries.

headline: The Great Northern Railway Arrives: Great Falls Joins the Continental Grid
summary:
## James J. Hill's Iron Road and the Making of a Commercial Hub

When Paris Gibson sought a partner to transform his hydroelectric dream into a working city, he turned to James J. Hill—the Canadian-born "Empire Builder" who was then laying track westward across the northern Great Plains toward the Pacific. Their alliance proved decisive. By 1887, Hill's rail lines had connected Great Falls to Helena and Butte, giving the young city its economic lifelines.

The Great Northern Railway's main transcontinental line, completed in 1893, passed north of Great Falls through Havre. But a branch line provided direct service to the city, and the combination of affordable freight rates and Hill's active promotion of agriculture and industry along his route transformed Great Falls into the premier trade center of north-central Montana. Grain from the vast wheat farms of the highline flowed through Great Falls elevators. Cattle from the open range came through Great Falls stockyards.

Hill's personal connection to Gibson—they had known each other during Gibson's Minnesota years—made Great Falls something of a favored project. Hill invested in the Great Falls Water Power and Townsite Company alongside Gibson and lobbied aggressively for federal land policies that encouraged agricultural settlement on the Montana plains.

The legacy of the Great Northern is visible today in the grain elevators, stockyards district, and the street layout of Great Falls' commercial core. The railroad that Hill built without government land grants—a rare achievement in the land-grant era—left its structural imprint on the city as surely as any single building.

headline: C. M. Russell Museum: Building a Legacy Around a Log Cabin Studio
summary:
## How Great Falls Became the Custodian of the Cowboy Artist's World

Charles Marion Russell built his log-cabin studio on Fourth Street North in Great Falls in 1903, and for the next twenty-three years it was the engine of his creative life. Twelve feet wide and twenty feet deep, with a north-facing skylight and walls covered in Native American artifacts, the studio was his sanctuary—off-limits to business talk, open to stories, laughter, and the company of old cowboys who dropped by to chew tobacco and remember the open range.

When Russell died in 1926, his wife Nancy worked to preserve the studio and the adjoining home, known as "Trail's End," understanding that the physical setting was as much a part of the Russell legacy as the paintings. The studio became a designated historic landmark. Over the following decades, Great Falls' civic leadership and cultural community organized around the goal of building a world-class museum worthy of Russell's stature.

The C. M. Russell Museum complex today occupies the block adjacent to the original studio and home. It holds the largest collection of Russell's original artwork in the world—paintings, bronze sculptures, illustrated letters, and personal effects—as well as archival materials documenting Montana's frontier era. The annual C. M. Russell Auction, held each March in Great Falls, is one of the premier Western art sales in the country.

The museum transformed Great Falls into a pilgrimage destination for Western art lovers and historians. What began as one man's log cabin studio became the anchor of a cultural identity that Great Falls has carried proudly into the twenty-first century.

headline: Gore Field and the Lend-Lease Air Bridge: Great Falls Goes to War
summary:
## How a Montana City Became the Pivot Point of an Unlikely Alliance

In the months after Pearl Harbor, Great Falls became the American nerve center of one of World War II's most consequential logistics operations. The Northwest Staging Route—a chain of airfields threading through Canada and Alaska to Fairbanks—was the conduit through which the United States delivered aircraft to the Soviet Union under the Lend-Lease program. Gore Field, on the west side of Great Falls, was where the pipeline began.

Bell P-39 Airacobras, Douglas C-47 Skytrains, North American B-25 Mitchells, and Douglas A-20 Havocs arrived at Gore Field by rail, were assembled, and were flown by American pilots to Fairbanks, where Soviet pilots took over for the final leg to Siberia. Over twenty-one months, approximately 8,000 aircraft made the journey. The operation's scale was staggering: 1,717,712 pounds of cargo—aircraft parts, explosives, medical supplies—were shipped through Great Falls to the Soviet Union.

Great Falls Army Air Base, just east of the city (later Malmstrom), handled the training and logistics support. The two bases together made Great Falls perhaps the most important single point in the entire Lend-Lease network. Historians have also noted that the operation's diplomatic channel was used to transfer sensitive documents and personnel in ways that remain subjects of ongoing historical inquiry.

The Lend-Lease operation ended in September 1945. Great Falls emerged from the war with two substantial military installations, a workforce accustomed to industrial production, and a strategic importance that would carry into the Cold War era.

headline: The Boston and Montana Smelter at Black Eagle: Wiring the World
summary:
## The Industrial Colossus That Made Great Falls the Electric City

In 1890, two years before Montana's statehood infrastructure had fully settled, the Boston and Montana Consolidated Copper and Silver Mining Company planted a smelter on a basalt bluff overlooking Black Eagle Dam north of Great Falls. It was the first major industrial facility in the city, and it would operate—under various corporate owners—for nearly ninety years.

The site was chosen for reasons that Paris Gibson had anticipated a decade earlier: the Missouri River dams could supply direct current electricity at costs unavailable anywhere else in the region. Black Eagle Dam, completed in 1891, ran power cables directly to the smelter's electric furnaces. The smelter refined raw copper ore from Butte into the wire that would help electrify American homes, power telegraph networks, and supply the munitions industry in both World Wars.

Ammalgamated Copper—later the Anaconda Copper Mining Company—absorbed the Boston and Montana operation by 1915. The company built the iconic 506-foot smokestack in 1908, and the community of Black Eagle grew up around it: company housing, a company school, six bars open round-the-clock. At its World War II peak, more than 4,000 workers labored on the hill.

The Great Falls smelter was not a smelter in its final decades but a refinery and wire mill—processing copper that had been smelted in Anaconda into finished industrial products. When the wire mill closed on July 1, 1980, and the stack came down in September 1981, it marked the end of the industrial era that had made Great Falls "the Electric City." The superfund cleanup of the former 405-acre site continues today.

headline: Gary Cooper — Helena's Favorite Son Who Became Hollywood Royalty
summary:
## From Central Grade School to Academy Award Glory

Frank James Cooper was born in Helena, Montana, on May 7, 1901, the younger son of English immigrant parents Charles and Alice Cooper. His father was a prominent Helena lawyer who rose to the Montana Supreme Court, and the family divided their time between the city and a 600-acre cattle ranch fifty miles north of Helena near Craig. Young Frank attended Central Grade School in Helena and rode horses on the family range—experiences that would later make him uniquely authentic as a screen cowboy.

Sent to school in England from 1909 to 1912, he returned to Helena and resumed his education at Johnson Grammar School before leaving Helena High School after two years in 1918. He drifted between the family ranch and Montana State College in Bozeman before heading to California in 1924 to seek work as an artist. Extra work in Westerns led to acting roles, and he adopted the name Gary Cooper.

Cooper's screen presence—quiet, physical, morally certain—made him the definitive American hero of his era. He won Academy Awards for Best Actor for *Sergeant York* (1941) and *High Noon* (1952). His career spanned more than 100 films over thirty-six years. When he received an honorary Oscar in 1961, his friend James Stewart broke down weeping during the tribute, unable to finish his speech.

Cooper died of prostate cancer in Beverly Hills on May 13, 1961. Helena claimed him forever: the city's pride in its most famous native son has never dimmed, and his portrait hangs in public buildings across the state.

headline: Myrna Loy — Helena Born, Hollywood Immortalized
summary:
## The "Perfect Wife" of American Cinema Grew Up Under the Big Sky

Myrna Adele Williams was born on August 2, 1905, in Helena, Montana, to a ranching family with roots in the valley south of the city. She spent her early years in rural Radersburg and in Helena itself before her father's death in 1918 set the family moving west to California. She carried Montana's clear-sky directness with her into the film world, where she became one of the most beloved actresses of Hollywood's golden age.

Renaming herself Myrna Loy for the screen, she transitioned from silent films to talkies with characteristic ease. Her partnership with William Powell across six *Thin Man* films (1934–1947) created the gold standard of sophisticated screen comedy—witty, equal, genuinely affectionate. She was named "the perfect wife" by audiences and press alike, a label she found both flattering and limiting. The American Film Institute later ranked her among the greatest female stars in cinema history.

Off screen, Loy was an activist decades before activism was fashionable for Hollywood stars. She worked for the American Red Cross during World War II, served UNESCO, and was a vocal advocate for civil rights and fair housing legislation. Her opposition to McCarthyism cost her professionally but not personally—she never recanted.

Loy died in New York City on December 14, 1993. The Myrna Loy Center for the Performing Arts in Helena—housed in the converted Lewis and Clark County Jail—stands as Montana's tribute to the actress who was born in the shadow of the Rocky Mountain Front and became an enduring figure of American popular culture.

headline: Samuel T. Hauser — Helena's Restless Captain of Industry
summary:
## Banker, Governor, Dam Builder—and Montana's Most Complicated Entrepreneur

Samuel Thomas Hauser arrived in Helena in 1866, two years after the gold strike at Last Chance Gulch, and immediately staked his future on the young city's economic vitality. Born in Missouri in 1833, he had drifted west during the Civil War and made his first banking connections in Virginia City before recognizing Helena as the more durable investment.

Hauser founded the First National Bank of Helena in 1866—the city's first chartered bank—and built it into a regional financial powerhouse with branches in Butte, Fort Benton, and Missoula. He was simultaneously a silver miner, railroad promoter, rancher, and smelter investor. His banking network financed much of Montana Territory's early infrastructure, including seven short-line railroads whose boards he sat on or chaired.

President Grover Cleveland appointed him the 7th Territorial Governor of Montana in 1885. Hauser served until 1887, often delegating gubernatorial duties so he could focus on business. He was a key figure in the Democratic Party coalition alongside William Clark and Marcus Daly, and fought hard to keep the state capital in Helena during the 1894 capital fight.

The Panic of 1893 wiped out most of his silver-era fortune. He rebuilt through hydroelectric power, financing the Hauser Dam on the Missouri—which famously burst in 1908, destroying his second fortune. He died in Helena on November 10, 1914, nearly broke but still celebrated as, in the words of one contemporary, "Montana's greatest captain of industry."

headline: Thomas Francis Meagher — The Irish General Who Governed Montana Territory
summary:
## From Exile to Acting Governor: An Irishman's Improbable Montana Chapter

Thomas Francis Meagher was born in Waterford, Ireland, in 1823 and became one of the most celebrated Irish nationalist leaders of his generation. Convicted of sedition against the British Crown, he was transported to Tasmania but escaped in 1852 and made his way to New York, where he trained as a lawyer and became a prominent figure in Irish-American political circles.

During the Civil War, Meagher raised and commanded the Irish Brigade—five regiments of Irish-American soldiers who distinguished themselves at Antietam, Fredericksburg, and Chancellorsville. After the war, President Andrew Johnson appointed him Secretary of the newly organized Montana Territory in 1865. When the first territorial governor departed, Meagher served as Acting Governor, navigating the raw frontier politics of a gold-rush territory with characteristic energy and occasional recklessness.

He arrived in Helena when it was barely a year old—a sprawling, muddy camp of saloons and sluice boxes—and tried to organize a functioning territorial government while managing conflicts between miners, ranchers, and the federal Indian Bureau. His tenure was controversial; he was accused of corruption by political enemies and celebrated by others as an energetic builder.

Meagher died in mysterious circumstances on July 1, 1867, falling from a riverboat into the Missouri River at Fort Benton. His body was never recovered. A bronze equestrian statue of him stands on the north lawn of the Montana State Capitol in Helena, placed there in 1905—a monument to the Irish revolutionary who spent his final chapter on the banks of the Missouri.

headline: Wilbur F. Sanders — Helena's Vigilante Statesman and First U.S. Senator
summary:
## The Man Who Said 'Men, Do Your Duty' and Built Montana's Legal Order

Wilbur Fisk Sanders arrived in Montana Territory in 1863 as a young Ohio lawyer accompanying his uncle, Sidney Edgerton, who was about to become the territory's first governor. He reached Bannack in time to confront one of the most dangerous criminal organizations in American frontier history—the road agent gang of Sheriff Henry Plummer—and stepped forward to prosecute the murderer George Ives before a miners' court, reportedly closing his argument with the phrase "Men, do your duty."

The Ives conviction set off the vigilante movement that broke Plummer's gang through a series of summary hangings in the winter of 1863–64. Sanders was the public face and legal conscience of the vigilantes, a role that defined him as a frontier lawman-lawyer of the first order. He moved with the capital from Bannack to Virginia City and finally to Helena in the 1870s, where he built a home, a law practice, and a political base.

For twenty-five years he served as president of the Montana Historical Society and helped found the Montana Bar Association. When statehood arrived in 1889, the Republican-controlled legislature elected him one of Montana's first two U.S. Senators. He served the short term—January 1890 to March 1893—and died in Helena in 1905.

A bronze statue of Sanders stands in the Montana State Capitol rotunda, and Sanders County bears his name. Few figures in Montana history bridged the raw justice of the vigilante era and the constitutional machinery of statehood as fully as this transplanted Ohio lawyer who chose Helena as his permanent home.

headline: Joseph K. Toole — Helena's First Governor at Montana Statehood
summary:
## The Democrat Who Led Montana Through Its First Decade of Statehood

Joseph Kemp Toole was born in Savannah, Missouri, in 1851 and came to Montana as a young lawyer in the 1870s, settling in Helena. He served as a delegate to Montana's constitutional convention in 1884 and was elected Montana's territorial delegate to Congress in 1884 and 1886—a position that allowed him to vote on legislation but not cast binding ballots, a frustration he channeled into relentless agitation for statehood.

When Montana was admitted to the Union on November 8, 1889, as the forty-first state, Toole was the natural choice for governor. He won election as Montana's first state governor and served from 1889 to 1893. Based in Helena, he presided over the creation of the state's executive branch, the establishment of state institutions, and the early battles over taxation of the mining industry that would define Montana politics for decades.

Toole was re-elected to a second gubernatorial term in 1901, serving until 1908, making him the only man to serve two non-consecutive terms as Montana's governor in the state's early history. His Helena law practice remained his professional base throughout his political career, and he was a member of the Democratic Party coalition that included copper magnates Marcus Daly and William Clark.

He died in Helena on March 11, 1929. As the man who served as Montana's first governor, Toole occupies a foundational place in the state's political genealogy—the elected embodiment of Helena's transformation from territorial capital to state capital.

headline: Thomas Cruse — The Unlettered Irishman Who Built Helena's Cathedral
summary:
## From Penniless Prospector to Helena's Most Generous Millionaire

Thomas Cruse was born in Ireland in 1836, arrived in Helena as a broke thirty-one-year-old prospector in July 1867, and spent the next eight years scrambling through the mountains northwest of the city before striking one of the richest gold and silver deposits in Montana history. On May 19, 1875, he staked the Drumlummon Mine—named after his Irish parish—and by 1883 had extracted enough ore to sell the property to English investors for $1.5 million cash plus retained shares. Total earnings to Cruse from the Drumlummon eventually approached $6 million.

Cruse never learned to read or write, yet he moved in the highest social circles of Helena and San Francisco, became a prominent banker and real estate developer, and married twenty-three-year-old Margaret Carter in 1886 in what newspapers called "the wedding of the century." He gave his bride $1 million as a wedding gift.

His most lasting civic contribution was the Cathedral of St. Helena. When Bishop John Carroll launched the project in 1905, Cruse donated the original $25,000 for the site and ultimately pledged more than $350,000 to construction—funding that made the cathedral possible. On December 20, 1914, five days before the first Mass was said in the completed building, Cruse died. His was the first funeral held in the cathedral he had built.

Cruse is buried in Helena, one of hundreds of prospectors who came to Last Chance Gulch dreaming of gold—and one of the handful who changed the city's skyline forever.

headline: Mike Mansfield — Raised in Great Falls, He Became Montana's Greatest Senator
summary: ## A Senator Who Shaped the Modern Congress from his Montana Roots

headline: Jeannette Rankin — Pioneer Congresswoman Born Near Helena
summary:
## The First Woman Elected to Congress Came From Montana's Capital Country

Jeannette Rankin was born near Missoula on June 11, 1880, but her political formation was inseparable from Helena and the state capital's corridors. After graduating from the University of Montana and working as a social worker in Seattle and New York, she returned to Montana in 1910 and led the statewide women's suffrage campaign, working alongside Helena suffragists to lobby the territorial legislature.

In 1916 she ran for and won a seat in the U.S. House of Representatives from Montana's at-large district, becoming the first woman ever elected to Congress—four years before women nationwide had the right to vote. Her votes, her speeches, and her office drew women's rights advocates from across Montana to Helena and Washington alike. She cast one of the fifty votes against U.S. entry into World War I in 1917.

After two decades out of Congress, Rankin returned for a second term in 1940 and cast the lone vote against declaring war on Japan on December 8, 1941—a principled stand that drew death threats and ended her political career but cemented her historical legacy. She was eighty-seven years old when she led a march of five thousand women against the Vietnam War in Washington in 1968.

Rankin died in California in 1973 at ninety-two. A bronze statue of her stands in the U.S. Capitol's National Statuary Hall, representing Montana. The Jeannette Rankin Foundation, headquartered in Missoula, continues her work by providing scholarships to nontraditional women college students.

headline: Charles M. Russell — The Artist Who Painted Helena's Capitol Chambers
summary:
## When the Cowboy Artist Received Montana's Most Prestigious Commission

Charles Marion Russell spent most of his adult life in Great Falls, but his most official artistic legacy hangs in Helena. In 1911, the Montana State Legislature selected three Montana artists to create murals for the newly expanded Capitol building: Ralph DeCamp for the Law Library, Edgar Paxson for the House Lobby, and Charles Russell for the House of Representatives Chamber itself.

Russell's contribution—*Lewis and Clark Meeting the Flathead Indians at Ross' Hole*—measures twelve by twenty-five feet and occupies the west wall above the speaker's platform. Painted in 1911 and installed the same year, it depicts the expedition's first encounter with the Salish people in the Bitterroot Valley, rendered with the ethnographic attention to dress, gear, and landscape that characterized Russell's finest work. It remains the most visited single artwork in Montana.

Russell's relationship with Helena extended beyond the Capitol commission. He exhibited here regularly, knew its merchant class personally, and spent time in the city on business and political errands. His advocacy for the Rocky Boy Reservation—successfully concluded in 1916—required lobbying in Helena as well as Washington.

The Capitol mural cements Russell's place not just as Great Falls' artist-in-residence but as Montana's official visual historian. Every session of the Montana Legislature convenes beneath his depiction of the moment European and Native American civilization first met in the Bitterroot—a reminder, painted in oil, of what came before.

headline: Last Chance Gulch: The Gold Strike That Built a Capital City
summary:
## How Four Desperate Prospectors Named Montana's Future Capital

On July 14, 1864, four weary prospectors—John Cowan of Georgia, D. J. Miller of Alabama, John Crabb of Iowa, and Reginald Stanley of England—made one final effort before giving up their search in what is now downtown Helena. They had been roaming western Montana for months, chasing gold strikes that petered out or proved already claimed. This gulch, they decided, was their last chance.

The placer gold they found in the stream that day set off one of the last great gold rushes of the American West. Within months, hundreds of miners had staked claims along Last Chance Gulch, and by the end of the 1860s the discoveries had produced an estimated $18 to $19 million in gold. The town that grew around the gulch was named Helena on October 30, 1864, after Helena, Minnesota, the home state of one of the founding miners.

Last Chance Gulch produced an estimated $3.6 billion in today's dollars over a twenty-year period—one of the richest placer deposits in American history. By 1888 the city had an estimated fifty millionaires, giving Helena the highest per-capita concentration of millionaires of any city in the world at that time.

The gulch itself became Helena's main commercial street—still named Last Chance Gulch today—where Victorian-era buildings rose on the same bedrock that miners had sluiced for color sixty years earlier. The name of a desperate expedient became the proud address of banks, theaters, and hotels.

headline: The Capital Fight of 1894: Helena Beats Anaconda by 1,906 Votes
summary:
## When Two Copper Kings Spent Millions to Decide Where Montana Would Govern Itself

In November 1894, Montana voters cast ballots on one of the most consequential questions in the state's young history: which city would be the permanent state capital? The contest pitted Helena—the territorial capital since 1875—against Anaconda, the company town built by copper magnate Marcus Daly around his massive smelter. It was, at its core, a proxy war between Daly and his bitter rival William Andrews Clark.

Daly wanted the capital in Anaconda, where he controlled the economy, the newspaper, and the social life. Clark had made a political alliance with Helena businessmen, promising to deliver the capital vote in exchange for their support of his U.S. Senate bid. Both men spent lavishly: Clark reportedly $500,000, Daly more than $2.5 million—staggering sums in a state where barely 52,000 men could vote. Free cigars, whiskey, and cash changed hands across the state.

Helena won by 27,024 to 25,118—a margin of 1,906 votes, or about 3.6 percent. Helena's capture of nearly 40 percent of the Butte vote, despite Daly's dominance there, proved decisive. The result secured Helena's position as the permanent seat of state government and ensured that its real estate, legal, and lobbying economy would continue to dominate Montana's civic life.

The capital fight remains the most expensive per-voter political campaign in nineteenth-century American state history. Its outcome was not just about geography—it determined which corporate interests would sit closest to the levers of state power for generations to come.

headline: The Montana State Capitol: From Scandal to Dome, 1896–1912
summary:
## Building a Symbol of Statehood in Copper Country

After the capital fight of 1894 settled Helena's primacy, the next challenge was building a Capitol worthy of Montana's ambitions. A Capitol Commission was formed in 1895, and in 1896 an architectural competition attracted designs from across the country. The commission selected St. Louis architect George R. Mann's grand design—then almost immediately became mired in corruption allegations.

When Mann's commission was disbanded in 1897, a second competition produced a winning design by the Iowa firm of Charles Bell and John Kent, who relocated to Helena to oversee construction. Their design—Greek neoclassical in Montana sandstone, with a copper-clad dome—was more modest than Mann's vision but more buildable. The cornerstone was laid on July 4, 1899, and the building was dedicated on July 4, 1902, though interior work continued for years.

In 1909 the legislature authorized major east and west wing expansions that more than doubled the building's floor space. The wings, completed in 1912, included new chambers for both houses of the legislature. The most celebrated additions were the murals commissioned for the interior: Charles M. Russell's *Lewis and Clark Meeting the Flathead Indians at Ross' Hole* went up in the House chamber in 1912, and Edgar Paxson's panoramic battle scene in the House lobby followed shortly after.

In 1934 the dome was re-clad in copper—a gift from Montana's mining industry to the seat of its governance. The Montana State Capitol is listed on the National Register of Historic Places and remains one of the finest Beaux-Arts public buildings in the Mountain West.

headline: The Cathedral of Saint Helena: Vienna's Gothic Revival in the Montana Rockies
summary:
## How Bishop Carroll Built a European Cathedral in an American Frontier Capital

In 1905, Bishop John Patrick Carroll of the Roman Catholic Diocese of Helena climbed Mount Helena to survey the city layout and choose a site for a new cathedral. He bought an entire city block on Lawrence Street for $25,000 donated by mining millionaire Thomas Cruse—and then set about building something that would rival the cathedrals of Europe.

The architect he chose was A. O. Von Herbulis, an Austro-Hungarian émigré trained in Vienna who modeled the design after the Votivkirche—one of Vienna's most celebrated neo-Gothic masterpieces. Construction began in 1908; the cornerstone was laid on October 4 of that year. The first Mass was celebrated on November 8, 1914. Final consecration came in June 1924, after sixteen years of construction and $645,000 in total cost.

The cathedral's most dramatic features are its twin 230-foot spires, topped with twelve-foot gold-leafed crosses. Inside, 59 stained glass windows totaling 11,693 square feet were crafted by the F. X. Zettler firm of Munich—a commission the firm itself called the finest windows it had produced in its first fifty years of operation. Twenty-nine exterior statues of saints in limestone complete a building that seems to have landed from the Ringstrasse in the middle of the Montana Rockies.

The cathedral suffered significant damage in the 1935 Helena earthquake, requiring reconstruction of the south tower. Added to the National Register of Historic Places in 1980, it remains the most architecturally ambitious building in Montana and one of the most striking Gothic Revival churches in the American West.

headline: Montana Statehood, 1889: Helena Enters the Union as Capital of the 41st State
summary:
## The Constitutional Convention That Turned a Territory Into a State

For more than two decades, Montana's territorial residents had chafed at their inability to elect their own governor or vote for their own congressional representatives. Gold rushes had built cities; copper had funded fortunes; railroads had connected the territory to the national economy. What remained missing was full political standing.

In the summer of 1889, seventy-five delegates gathered in Helena for the constitutional convention that would write the document enabling Montana's admission to the Union. They were a cross-section of the territory's power: mine owners, ranchers, merchants, and lawyers. The biggest fights were over legislative representation (population-based versus county-based), taxation of mines, and women's suffrage—all three battles ultimately won by the mining and conservative interests.

The constitution they produced taxed mines only on the value of their original $5 claims—even claims producing millions annually—a provision that constrained state revenues and favored the Copper Kings for decades. Women's suffrage was defeated 43 to 25. On November 8, 1889, President Grover Cleveland signed the proclamation admitting Montana as the forty-first state. Helena became its capital, and the first state legislature convened in temporary quarters while the Capitol building awaited construction.

The statehood convention's compromises shaped Montana politics for generations: a state born with one hand tied behind its back by corporate tax exemptions, its women disenfranchised for another twenty-five years, and its Democratic and Republican parties already locked in the copper-financed warfare that would define the Gilded Age.

headline: The 1935 Helena Earthquake: When the Cathedral's Tower Fell
summary:
## A Series of Tremors That Shook Montana's Capital to Its Foundation

In the fall of 1935, a series of earthquakes rattled the Helena valley in the most destructive seismic event in Montana's recorded history. The most severe tremors struck on October 12 and October 31, registering in the magnitude 6 range. Two people were killed directly, dozens were injured, and an estimated $4 million in damage—an enormous sum during the Depression—was inflicted on the city's building stock.

The Cathedral of Saint Helena bore some of the most visible damage. Its south tower was nearly completely destroyed, and the masonry walls sustained cracks that required years of repair. The tower was reconstructed and structurally reinforced by 1938. At the Montana State Capitol, the original glass floor of the rotunda shattered, and the walls' plaster and fresco designs were damaged extensively—damage that was patched using WPA labor in the following year.

Residential Helena suffered heavily. Chimneys collapsed across the city, and unreinforced masonry buildings throughout the commercial district showed cracking and partial failure. The earthquake retrofit of the Capitol building—earthquake engineering was in its infancy—would not be completed until the 1950s and 1960s.

The 1935 quakes remain the benchmark against which Helena's seismic vulnerability is measured. They accelerated a shift toward reinforced construction in public buildings and remain a reminder that Helena sits atop a seismically active zone—a fact the European-trained architects who built the cathedral's soaring Gothic towers had no reason to anticipate.

headline: Carroll College: Helena's Catholic Liberal Arts Anchor Since 1909
summary:
## The College on the Hill That Almost Became the Capitol

Carroll College was founded in 1909 by Bishop John Carroll of the Roman Catholic Diocese of Helena—the same bishop who was simultaneously overseeing construction of the Cathedral of Saint Helena. The site he chose was the promontory on the west side of Helena that had been seriously considered for the Montana State Capitol itself; developer Peter Winne's competing offer of free land on the east side had diverted the Capitol to its current location in 1895.

Carroll opened as Mount St. Charles College—a small Catholic men's school—with Bishop Carroll's mandate to provide a liberal arts education rooted in Catholic intellectual tradition to the children of Helena's working class and Montana's farming and mining communities. It was renamed Carroll College in 1932 in honor of its founding bishop.

Through the first half of the twentieth century Carroll grew steadily, becoming coeducational, expanding its curriculum into nursing and education, and building the distinctive brick campus that today overlooks the city from its western ridge. Its nursing program became one of the most respected in the region, and its pre-health sciences track produced generations of Montana physicians.

Carroll's Fighting Saints athletic program has won multiple NAIA national championships across multiple sports, earning the college national recognition disproportionate to its enrollment of roughly 1,400 students. The college's presence on Helena's west side has anchored a neighborhood of hospitals, health facilities, and educational institutions, making it as much a civic institution as an academic one.

headline: The Mansion District: Helena's Millionaires' Row and the Gilded Age Legacy
summary:
## When Helena Had More Millionaires Per Capita Than Anywhere on Earth

In the 1880s and 1890s, Helena accumulated wealth at a rate that astonished even the Gilded Age. The gold of Last Chance Gulch, the silver of surrounding mountains, and the copper profits flowing through Helena banks created a class of plutocrats who expressed their success in brick and sandstone along the avenues above the gulch. By 1888 the city was home to an estimated fifty millionaires—a per-capita concentration that contemporary observers calculated exceeded any city in the world.

The mansions they built along Stuart, Madison, and Benton avenues followed Victorian and Romanesque Revival fashions then current in Chicago and New York: steep gables, turrets, wrap-around porches, ornate woodwork, and formal grounds. The Hauser house, the Sanders house (built 1875), and the Broadwater Hotel and Natatorium—opened in 1889 by Helena banker Charles Broadwater as Montana's most luxurious resort—defined a social landscape unique in the Mountain West.

The Panic of 1893, which crushed silver prices and wiped out many Montana fortunes, began the slow erosion of the mansion district's original opulence. Some properties were subdivided; others fell into boarding houses. But a substantial number survived intact, and today Helena's mansion district—listed on the National Register of Historic Places—remains one of the most intact Victorian residential concentrations in the Mountain West.

The district is anchored by the Montana Historical Society museum and the Original Governor's Mansion (built 1888), which served as the official residence of Montana's governors until 1959.

headline: The Northern Pacific Railroad Arrives in Helena, 1883: From Boom Town to Capital City
summary:
## The Iron Road That Turned Helena's Gold-Rush Economy Into Something Permanent

By 1883, Last Chance Gulch's placer gold was largely exhausted, and Helena faced the classic fate of gold-rush towns: abandonment. What saved it was the arrival, in that same year, of the Northern Pacific Railroad—the transcontinental line that crossed Montana on its way from the Great Lakes to Puget Sound. Helena, strategically located in the valley east of the Continental Divide, found itself on the main line.

The railroad transformed Helena's economic base from extraction to distribution and administration. Where Helena had been a supply depot for surrounding mining camps, it now became a wholesale center, a financial hub, and—increasingly—the political and bureaucratic heart of Montana Territory. The railroad brought capital, manufactured goods, and people at a scale the gold camps had never seen.

Marcus Daly's Anaconda smelter opened in 1883 as well, and the Northern Pacific carried copper ore from Butte through Helena's commercial sphere. The territorial capital designation, which Helena had held since 1875, took on new permanence as the rail connection cemented the city's role as the distribution point for the entire territory. Lawyers, bankers, and lobbyists followed the railroad to Helena.

When Montana became the forty-first state in 1889, Helena's position was unassailable. The capital fight of 1894 ratified what the railroad had already made functionally true: Helena was where Montana's business got done, where its money flowed, and where its political future would be decided.

headline: The Walking City: How Helena's Downtown Rose Directly from the Gulch
summary:
## Last Chance Gulch as Street, Market, and Memory

Helena's downtown has one of the most peculiar layouts in American urban history: its main commercial street follows the sinuous path of an old mining gulch, bending and curving where Victorian miners once sluiced for placer gold. Last Chance Gulch, as the street is still called today, runs roughly north-south through the heart of the city, its irregular course a permanent memorial to the geological accident that created Helena.

Buildings went up along the gulch as miners gave way to merchants, bankers, and attorneys. By the 1880s, Helena's commercial district was one of the most densely developed in the Mountain West, with Victorian brick buildings housing banks, newspaper offices, hotels, and specialty retailers catering to a wealthy clientele. The Montana Club, founded in 1885 as an elite private club, occupied an imposing building on Last Chance Gulch and served as the social hub of the state's mining and railroad aristocracy.

A catastrophic fire swept through Helena in 1874, destroying much of the wooden commercial district and compelling a rebuilding in brick that gave the downtown its Victorian architectural character. The rebuilt city was larger, denser, and more permanent than the gold-rush camp it replaced.

Helena's pedestrian walking mall, established in the late twentieth century, converted part of Last Chance Gulch into a landscaped promenade—preserving the Victorian storefronts while adapting the street for modern use. Walking its irregular course today, visitors still follow the path of the four Georgians' 1864 discovery, though the water runs underground now and the gold ran out long ago.

headline: Charles E. Conrad — The Freighter Who Founded Kalispell
summary:
## From the Missouri River to the Flathead: A Pioneer's Final Chapter

Charles Edward Conrad was born in Virginia in 1850 and came to Montana as a young man after the Civil War. Working as a clerk and eventually co-owner of a Fort Benton freighting firm with his brother William, he built one of the largest trading operations on the Missouri River, supplying military posts, Native communities, and frontier settlements across the northern plains. He grew rich and became known across the territory for his ability to negotiate with Indian tribes—a reputation that earned him a role mediating between the British government and five tribal groups after the Nez Perce War of 1877.

In the fall of 1890, Conrad journeyed the length of Flathead Lake on his way to Spokane and recognized in the Flathead Valley an opportunity that Fort Benton could no longer offer. Learning that the Great Northern Railway would route its transcontinental line through the valley, he organized the Kalispell Townsite Company in 1891 with several railroad associates and platted a new city at the division point the railroad had chosen.

Kalispell was born in 1891, and Conrad became its most prominent citizen—founding the Conrad National Bank, investing in ranching, real estate, and mining, and building the 13,000-square-foot Victorian mansion that still stands today as a museum. He also assembled a bison herd on his seventy-two acres that later helped establish the National Bison Range in Moiese.

Conrad died in his Kalispell bedroom on November 28, 1902. His daughter Alicia, who lived in the mansion until her death, donated it to the city of Kalispell in 1974—ensuring that the founder's home would remain the city's most visited landmark.

headline: T. J. Hileman — Kalispell Photographer Who Captured Glacier's First Glory
summary:
## The Man Whose Camera Introduced Glacier National Park to the World

Thorstrand Jefferson Hileman was born in 1882 and moved to Kalispell, Montana, in 1911 to open his own portrait studio. He was already an accomplished photographer, but it was his proximity to the newly designated Glacier National Park—established in 1910, the year before his arrival—that would define his legacy.

In 1924 the Great Northern Railway appointed Hileman its official photographer, a position that sent him into Glacier's valleys, passes, and glacial lakes with a commercial mission: to create images dramatic enough to sell tourists on the journey west. The images he produced far exceeded that mandate. His photographs of Many Glacier, Lake McDonald, the Garden Wall, and the Going-to-the-Sun corridor introduced millions of Americans to one of the continent's most spectacular landscapes.

Hileman opened photo-finishing labs at both Glacier Park Lodge and Many Glacier Hotel in 1926, making his work available to tourists as souvenirs and to magazines as editorial material. His pre–World War II archive of Glacier photographs—shot on large-format glass plates and film—is described by historians as an unparalleled body of work documenting the park in its early decades, before mass automobile tourism transformed the visitor experience.

He spent most of his life in Kalispell, working from his studio and making seasonal forays into the park. The Northwest Montana History Museum in Kalispell holds significant portions of his archive and has undertaken ongoing preservation of his glass-plate negatives. Hileman died in 1945, having done more than any other individual to fix Glacier National Park in the American visual imagination.

headline: Dorothy Johnson — Western Fiction Writer Who Grew Up in Kalispell's Shadow
summary:
## The Author Behind *The Man Who Shot Liberty Valance* Called Whitefish Home—and Kalispell Her First Newsroom

Dorothy Marie Johnson was born in Iowa in 1905 and moved with her family to Whitefish, Montana—just twenty miles north of Kalispell—in 1913. She grew up in the Flathead Valley and graduated from Whitefish High School in 1922. Her first professional writing job came as a teenage stringer for the *Daily Inter Lake* in Kalispell, where she developed the habit of close observation that would distinguish her prose.

After studying at the University of Montana, Johnson spent two decades working as an editor in New York, writing western fiction on the side. Her short stories—published in the *Saturday Evening Post* and other major magazines—reimagined the American West with unsentimental clarity, centering the experiences of women, Native Americans, and ordinary frontier settlers rather than romanticized gunfighters.

Three of her stories became landmark films: *The Hanging Tree* (1959, starring Gary Cooper), *The Man Who Shot Liberty Valance* (1962, starring John Wayne and James Stewart), and *A Man Called Horse* (1970). She returned to Montana permanently in the early 1950s, joining the journalism faculty at the University of Montana in Missoula.

Johnson was adopted by the Blackfeet tribe and given the name Kills Both Places. She died on November 11, 1984. Her tombstone, per her instructions, reads "PAID"—a final sardonic joke from a writer who spent years paying off a deadbeat husband's gambling debts. She remains one of the most important western fiction writers of the twentieth century.

headline: Brad Bird — The Kalispell Kid Who Became Pixar's Master Storyteller
summary:
## From a Montana Mountain Town to Two Academy Awards at Pixar

Philip Bradley Bird was born on September 24, 1957, in Kalispell, Montana, the youngest of four children. His grandfather, Francis Wesley Bird, was a president of Montana Power Company. Young Brad developed an obsession with animation by age eleven, completed his first short film at fourteen, and sent it to Walt Disney Productions—which led to an apprenticeship under the studio's legendary "Nine Old Men."

Bird attended the California Institute of the Arts and spent years in Hollywood's development trenches before landing a position as executive consultant on *The Simpsons* in its first eight seasons. His 1999 film *The Iron Giant*—emotionally rich, visually inventive, and a box-office disappointment—nonetheless built his reputation as a filmmaker of rare craft.

At Pixar he wrote and directed *The Incredibles* (2004) and *Ratatouille* (2007), winning Academy Awards for Best Animated Feature for both films. He also received Best Original Screenplay nominations for each. *Mission: Impossible – Ghost Protocol* (2011), his live-action debut, became the highest-grossing film in that franchise at the time. *Incredibles 2* (2018) became the second-highest-grossing animated film of all time during its theatrical run.

Bird holds the record for the most Annie Award wins in animation history. He is regarded as one of the most technically ambitious and thematically serious filmmakers working in any medium. His birthplace of Kalispell has little outward trace of his early life, but his grandfather's Montana Power lineage ran through the same hydroelectric veins that powered the region's growth.

headline: Lily Gladstone — Kalispell Born, History Made at the Golden Globes
summary:
## The First Indigenous Actress to Win a Golden Globe Began in Flathead Valley

Lily Gladstone was born on August 2, 1986, in Kalispell, Montana, and grew up partly on the Blackfeet Reservation near Browning before her family moved to the Seattle area during her middle school years. She returned to Montana for college, studying acting at the University of Montana in Missoula, where she developed the quietly powerful physical presence that would define her screen performances.

Her early film work—particularly Kelly Reichardt's *Certain Women* (2016), in which she played a ranch hand with longing and restraint that critics called one of the best performances of the year—built a devoted following without bringing mainstream recognition. That changed decisively in 2023, when Martin Scorsese cast her as Mollie Burkhart in *Killers of the Flower Moon*—the Osage woman at the center of the 1920s Oklahoma murder conspiracy that Scorsese brought to the screen after decades of planning.

Gladstone's performance earned her a Golden Globe for Best Actress in a Motion Picture Drama—the first Indigenous actor to win the award in the Golden Globes' eighty-one-year history. She was subsequently nominated for the Academy Award for Best Actress, becoming the first Native American actress of American descent to receive that nomination.

She has spoken publicly about the importance of authentic Indigenous representation in Hollywood and has used her platform to advocate for Native communities. Kalispell and the Flathead Valley claim her as one of their own, a reminder that the most transformative talents sometimes emerge from the most unexpected places.

headline: John E. Erickson — Kalispell's Governor Who Became a U.S. Senator
summary:
## The Norwegian-American Lawyer Who Rose from Flathead County to Washington

John Edward Erickson was born in Stoughton, Wisconsin, in 1863, of Norwegian immigrant parentage, and came to Montana as a young lawyer in the 1890s, settling in Kalispell. He built his law practice in the Flathead Valley and became deeply embedded in the civic and Democratic Party politics of northwest Montana, serving as county attorney for Flathead County before seeking higher office.

Elected Governor of Montana in 1924, Erickson served two terms until 1933, guiding the state through the economic turbulence of the late 1920s and the early Depression years. His administration was marked by efforts to maintain state services under severe revenue constraints as Montana's agricultural and mining economies contracted.

In 1933, following the death of U.S. Senator Thomas Walsh, Governor Erickson appointed himself to fill the vacant seat—an act of considerable political audacity that drew criticism but was legally within his authority. He served in the Senate until 1934, when he was defeated in a primary election. He returned to Kalispell and continued his legal practice until his death in 1946.

Erickson's career traced a path characteristic of the Flathead Valley's immigrant-stock professional class: Norwegian roots, legal training, Democratic Party loyalty, and a steady ascent through Montana's political hierarchy. The Kalispell he represented had grown from a railroad town into the commercial hub of a region defined by agriculture, timber, and the emerging tourism economy of Glacier National Park.

headline: Margaret Qualley — Kalispell Born Actress of *The Substance* and *Once Upon a Time*
summary:
## Andie MacDowell's Daughter Found Her Own Hollywood Star Shining Bright

Sarah Margaret Qualley was born on October 23, 1994, in Kalispell, Montana, to actress Andie MacDowell and rancher-model Paul Qualley. She spent her early years in the Flathead Valley before her family's professional obligations drew them south and east. She trained as a ballet dancer in her youth and pursued acting through the family's entertainment connections.

Qualley made her screen debut in the 2013 James Franco film *Palo Alto* and built her profile steadily, appearing in *The Nice Guys* (2016) and Quentin Tarantino's *Once Upon a Time in Hollywood* (2019), where her role as Pussycat—a Manson Family member who catches Brad Pitt's character's eye on the Sunset Strip—drew widespread critical attention. Her performance in the Netflix miniseries *Maid* (2021), in which she played a domestic abuse survivor navigating poverty and the welfare system, earned Emmy nominations and cemented her as one of her generation's most compelling dramatic performers.

Her role in the body-horror film *The Substance* (2024) earned her a Golden Globe nomination and renewed critical acclaim, establishing her as a performer willing to take physical and conceptual risks that most Hollywood actresses avoid. She married musician Jack Antonoff in August 2023.

Qualley's Kalispell birthright situates her alongside Lily Gladstone as part of a remarkable generation of serious, award-recognized actresses who trace their origins to the Flathead Valley—a geographic coincidence that says something about the depth of Montana's creative soil.

headline: Brock Osweiler — Kalispell's Super Bowl Champion Quarterback
summary:
## Flathead High School's Finest NFL Product Won It All with Denver

Brock Alan Osweiler was born in Coeur d'Alene, Idaho, on November 22, 1990, but was raised in Kalispell, Montana, and attended Flathead High School, where he became one of the most heavily recruited high school quarterbacks in the country. At six feet seven inches, he combined a rare physical frame with an arm capable of throwing the deep ball on the high-altitude Montana fields where he developed his game.

He chose Arizona State University and became a consensus first-team All-Pac-12 quarterback in 2011. The Denver Broncos selected him in the second round of the 2012 NFL draft. For three seasons he developed behind Peyton Manning—learning from one of football's greatest students of the game—before taking over the starting role in November 2015 when Manning was injured.
